module.label: Pediatric headache, seizure, altered mental status, or focal neurologic concern

use_when: Child or adolescent with headache, first or recurrent seizure, blackout with seizure markers, altered conscious state, persistent post-ictal state, focal neurologic symptoms, papilledema concern, raised intracranial pressure concern, or neurologic symptoms after head injury.

Pediatric neuro workup needs age-band routing, baseline neurologic status, bedside glucose, seizure/status escalation, headache red flags, infection or head-injury routing, focused neurologic maneuvers, and imaging/EEG stewardship.
do_not_use_when: Adult patient; use the adult stroke, TIA, seizure, headache, or altered mental status pathway as appropriate.
Unstable child with active seizure, airway compromise, shock, severe trauma, suspected sepsis, or rapidly falling conscious state; escalate immediately while documenting focused findings.
Primary syncope/chest pain without seizure markers or neurologic symptoms; use the pediatric chest pain/syncope/palpitations pathway.
Primary fever without headache, seizure, altered mental status, meningism, or focal neurologic symptoms; use the pediatric fever/sepsis pathway.
Known specialty neurology, neurosurgery, oncology, transplant, metabolic, or epilepsy protocol where a local specialty pathway overrides this general pediatric emergency neuro module.

modifier_add_on_policy.rationale: This validated pediatric neuro pack already includes fever, meningitis, encephalitis, sepsis, rash, travel, and infection escalation; generic infection-source prompts should not override the neuro-specific source-localizing history.

differentialBuckets.label: Primary headache or migraine only after pediatric red flags are absent
differentialBuckets.action: Classify as lower risk only when the child has no red-flag history, normal neurologic examination, normal blood pressure for age, no concerning age band, and reliable follow-up.
differentialBuckets.rationale: RCH states investigations are not routinely indicated for children with headache who have no red-flag features on history or examination.

source.implementation_notes: Do not let a migraine label suppress red-flag screening or age-specific referral rules.

differentialBuckets.diagnostic_target: Migraine, tension-type headache, or other primary headache pattern.
differentialBuckets.management_change: Changes workup toward symptom care, trigger review, medication-overuse review, school/social impact assessment, follow-up, and safety net instead of routine imaging or broad labs.
differentialBuckets.likelihood_ratio_note: Likelihood ratios are not available for this broad low-risk headache frame; guideline-defined red flags and examination findings determine whether reassurance is safe.
differentialBuckets.limitations: A primary headache pattern is not enough to discharge when the child is under 4 years, has progressive or sleep-waking headache, vomiting, altered consciousness, focal signs, papilledema, trauma, fever with meningism, shunt, malignancy, or immunosuppression.

differentialBuckets.label: Secondary headache, raised intracranial pressure, infection, or head injury
differentialBuckets.action: Screen for age under 4 years, morning or sleep-waking headache, progressive worsening, cough/positional trigger, sudden severe onset, occipital pain, vomiting, focal neurologic symptoms, papilledema, meningism, head injury, shunt, malignancy, immunosuppression, hypertension, anticoagulants, or bleeding tendency.
differentialBuckets.rationale: RCH and NICE define pediatric headache red flags that require senior review, same-day assessment, or consideration of intracranial imaging.

source.source_section: headaches in children under 12 and recurrent headache recommendations
source.implementation_notes: Use local ED/neuroimaging pathway when same-day assessment criteria are met.

differentialBuckets.diagnostic_target: Raised intracranial pressure, tumor, hydrocephalus, meningitis, encephalitis, intracranial abscess, head injury, hypertensive headache, shunt complication, or systemic disease.
differentialBuckets.management_change: Changes urgency of senior review, neuroimaging discussion, lumbar puncture safety, infection testing, antibiotics/antivirals, head injury pathway, admission, transfer, and discharge constraints.
differentialBuckets.likelihood_ratio_note: A single LR is not available for the combined secondary headache frame; red flags route to urgent assessment because multiple high-risk etiologies are possible.
differentialBuckets.limitations: Normal early appearance does not remove the need for assessment when source-defined red flags are present.

differentialBuckets.label: Seizure, status epilepticus, or post-ictal neurologic risk
differentialBuckets.action: Determine whether the child is actively seizing, has had repeated seizures without return to baseline, had a first afebrile seizure, has focal features, is under 12 months, has metabolic or toxicologic risk, or has not returned to baseline.
differentialBuckets.rationale: RCH and CHQ treat seizure lasting more than 5 minutes or repeated seizures without recovery as status epilepticus and a neurologic emergency.

source.source_section: key points, assessment, and emergency management
source.implementation_notes: Active seizure care should run through local resuscitation/status epilepticus protocol, not checklist pacing.

differentialBuckets.diagnostic_target: Status epilepticus, first afebrile seizure, complex febrile seizure, focal seizure, metabolic seizure, toxicologic seizure, epilepsy syndrome, or seizure mimic.
differentialBuckets.management_change: Changes airway support, benzodiazepine and second-line therapy timing, glucose testing, labs, ECG, neuroimaging, EEG/referral timing, admission, and transfer threshold.
differentialBuckets.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to the status epilepticus emergency rule; seizure duration, recovery, and physiologic compromise define management.
differentialBuckets.limitations: Do not diagnose epilepsy solely from one ED event; specialist assessment, witness history, ECG, and EEG/imaging decisions depend on source-defined features.

differentialBuckets.label: Altered conscious state from trauma, infection, metabolic, toxic, seizure, or hypoxic cause
differentialBuckets.action: Treat any GCS below 15, abnormal AVPU response, persistent confusion, coma, or caregiver-reported altered baseline as management-changing until immediate causes are addressed.
differentialBuckets.rationale: RCH notes altered conscious state in children is uncommon but associated with significant morbidity and mortality, and immediate management aims to minimize ongoing neurologic damage.

source.source_section: key points, causes, history, examination, investigations, and treatment
source.implementation_notes: Parents and nurses are often the best judge of change from baseline, especially for children with disability.

differentialBuckets.diagnostic_target: Post-ictal state, meningitis, encephalitis, sepsis, head injury, poisoning, hypoglycemia, DKA, electrolyte disorder, hypoxia, shock, stroke, intracranial hemorrhage, shunt failure, or safeguarding concern.
differentialBuckets.management_change: Changes resuscitation priority, POCT glucose, infection/toxin/metabolic tests, imaging, lumbar puncture safety, antibiotics/antivirals, senior review, admission, and transfer.
differentialBuckets.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not available for altered conscious state as a syndrome; management is driven by physiologic risk and source-specific causes.
differentialBuckets.limitations: Do not discharge if conscious state has not returned to normal or the cause remains unexplained.

differentialBuckets.label: Focal neurologic deficit, stroke mimic, or pediatric stroke concern
differentialBuckets.action: Document onset time, focal deficit pattern, seizure relationship, headache/trauma/infection context, sickle cell or prothrombotic risk, and whether deficits persist after recovery.
differentialBuckets.rationale: Pediatric focal deficits require child-specific evaluation and imaging/specialist routing rather than adult-only stroke scores.

source.source_section: imaging indications after seizure and consultation criteria
source.implementation_notes: Persistent focal signs after a seizure should not be dismissed as a typical post-ictal state without senior review.

differentialBuckets.diagnostic_target: Pediatric stroke, intracranial hemorrhage, Todd paresis, mass lesion, seizure focus, migraine variant, infection, trauma, or metabolic mimic.
differentialBuckets.management_change: Changes onset documentation, urgent senior/neurology review, neuroimaging, glucose and metabolic correction, seizure management, transfer, and activity/disposition restrictions.
differentialBuckets.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not available because pediatric focal deficit workup depends on age, onset, seizure context, and imaging; adult scores are not used.
differentialBuckets.limitations: This module is not a thrombolysis protocol and must follow local pediatric stroke or retrieval pathways when stroke is suspected.

safetyChecks.label: Measure temperature
safetyChecks.action: Measure and document current temperature with age-appropriate method.
safetyChecks.rationale: Fever changes headache, seizure, or altered mental status toward meningitis, encephalitis, sepsis, or febrile seizure pathways.
safetyChecks.management_change: Fever changes need for infection-source review, cultures/labs, antibiotics/antivirals, lumbar-puncture safety assessment, admission, and transfer.
safetyChecks.options: ___ C/F / Febrile / Afebrile / Hypothermic / Not available
safetyChecks.limitations: Antipyretics and timing can mask fever trajectory.
safetyChecks.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to temperature as baseline clinical data.

source.implementation_notes: Keep temperature in baseline data, not physical exam maneuvers.

safetyChecks.label: Measure heart rate
safetyChecks.action: Measure and document heart rate with age-band interpretation and rhythm impression.
safetyChecks.rationale: Tachycardia, bradycardia, or irregular rhythm can signal sepsis, shock, raised intracranial pressure, toxin, arrhythmia mimic, or post-seizure instability.
safetyChecks.management_change: Abnormal heart rate changes monitoring, ECG, resuscitation, poison/metabolic workup, senior review, and transfer threshold.
safetyChecks.options: ___ bpm / age-appropriate / tachycardic / bradycardic / irregular / not available
safetyChecks.limitations: Pain, fever, crying, medications, and post-ictal state affect interpretation.
safetyChecks.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to heart rate as baseline safety data.

source.source_section: examination: cardiac and raised intracranial pressure signs
source.implementation_notes: Bradycardia with hypertension is a late raised-ICP sign and should trigger urgent escalation.

safetyChecks.label: Measure respiratory rate
safetyChecks.action: Measure respiratory rate and respiratory pattern.
safetyChecks.rationale: Respiratory depression, irregular respirations, hypoxia, or shock can drive altered mental status and determine seizure medication safety.
safetyChecks.management_change: Abnormal respiratory status changes airway support, oxygen, blood gas, senior review, ICU or transfer threshold, and medication timing.
safetyChecks.options: ___ /min / age-appropriate / tachypnea / bradypnea / irregular / apnea concern / not available
safetyChecks.limitations: Crying, sedation, seizure activity, and post-ictal state affect interpretation.
safetyChecks.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to respiratory rate as baseline safety data.

source.implementation_notes: Airway and breathing concerns take priority over checklist completion.

safetyChecks.label: Measure blood pressure
safetyChecks.action: Measure blood pressure and interpret against pediatric age/height context when available.
safetyChecks.rationale: Hypertension can cause headache and can be a raised-ICP clue when paired with bradycardia; hypotension suggests shock or sepsis.
safetyChecks.management_change: Abnormal BP changes resuscitation, raised-ICP concern, hypertensive emergency workup, medication/toxin review, and disposition.
safetyChecks.options: ___/___ mmHg / age-appropriate / hypertensive / hypotensive / orthostatic concern / not available
safetyChecks.limitations: Incorrect cuff size and agitation can distort measurements; repeat abnormal values.
safetyChecks.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to blood pressure as baseline safety data.

source.source_section: recurrent headaches and blood pressure recommendation
source.implementation_notes: Headache workups need BP with pediatric interpretation rather than adult-only thresholds.

safetyChecks.label: Measure oxygen saturation
safetyChecks.action: Measure oxygen saturation and document oxygen delivery or room air.
safetyChecks.rationale: Hypoxemia can cause altered mental status, worsen seizures, and change airway/transfer decisions.
safetyChecks.management_change: Low saturation triggers oxygen, airway/breathing reassessment, respiratory source review, senior review, and transfer threshold.
safetyChecks.options: ___% room air / ___% on oxygen / low / normal / not available
safetyChecks.limitations: Motion, poor perfusion, nail products, and seizure activity can impair readings.
safetyChecks.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to oxygen saturation as baseline safety data.

source.implementation_notes: Oxygen saturation is baseline safety data, not a physical exam maneuver.

safetyChecks.label: Document mental status with AVPU or pediatric GCS
safetyChecks.action: Document AVPU and formal pediatric GCS when any impairment or caregiver-reported altered baseline is present.
safetyChecks.rationale: RCH defines altered conscious state with GCS below 15 and notes any AVPU impairment should prompt formal GCS.
safetyChecks.management_change: Abnormal or falling score changes resuscitation priority, imaging, LP safety, antibiotics/antivirals, senior review, admission, and transfer.
safetyChecks.options: Alert / responds to voice / responds to pain / unresponsive / GCS ___ / baseline differs / not available
safetyChecks.findings_options: AVPU alert / AVPU responds to voice / AVPU responds to pain / AVPU unresponsive / pediatric GCS ___ / caregiver says baseline differs / not available
safetyChecks.limitations: Developmental delay, sedation, post-ictal state, intoxication, and language barriers affect scoring.
safetyChecks.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to conscious-state scoring as baseline safety data.

source.implementation_notes: Parents are often the best judge of altered baseline, especially for children with disability.

safetyChecks.label: Measure bedside glucose
safetyChecks.action: Check point-of-care glucose for seizure, altered mental status, weakness, suspected metabolic cause, diabetes, poor intake, or unexplained neuro symptoms.
safetyChecks.rationale: RCH and NICE identify hypoglycemia/metabolic disturbance as a seizure and altered-conscious-state mimic that must be corrected promptly.
safetyChecks.management_change: Abnormal glucose changes immediate treatment, DKA/metabolic workup, seizure management, admission, and safe discharge.
safetyChecks.options: ___ mg/dL or mmol/L / low / high / normal / not available
safetyChecks.limitations: Confirm unexpected values per local policy without delaying treatment of severe hypoglycemia.
safetyChecks.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to bedside glucose as baseline safety data; abnormal results directly change management.

source.implementation_notes: All actively seizing or post-seizure children need rapid glucose availability.

safetyChecks.label: Measure current weight
safetyChecks.action: Document current measured or best estimated weight in kg.
safetyChecks.rationale: Pediatric seizure medicines, fluids, analgesia, antimicrobials, and transfer handoffs are weight-based.
safetyChecks.management_change: Missing weight blocks safe dosing for benzodiazepines, second-line antiseizure agents, fluids, analgesia, antimicrobials, and sedation planning.
safetyChecks.options: ___ kg measured / ___ kg estimated / Broselow or local estimate / not available
safetyChecks.limitations: Use actual weight when possible and document if estimated during resuscitation.
safetyChecks.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to weight as baseline dosing data.

source.implementation_notes: Weight is baseline safety data, not physical exam.

requiredQuestions.label: Ask age and neurodevelopment baseline
requiredQuestions.text: What is the child's exact age, neurodevelopmental baseline, communication baseline, seizure history, disability baseline, and usual mobility or behavior?
requiredQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask first for every pediatric neuro concern.
requiredQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Age and baseline change headache referral thresholds, seizure risk, GCS interpretation, behavior change interpretation, and discharge safety.
requiredQuestions.management_implication: Positive infant, under-4 headache, developmental-delay, disability, or changed-baseline context changes senior review, testing, admission, follow-up, and adult-pathway exclusion.
requiredQuestions.options: Multi-select: under 6 months / 6 to 23 months / 2 to 3 years / 4 to 11 years / 12 years or older / known developmental difference / known epilepsy / baseline changed / baseline unchanged
requiredQuestions.likelihood_ratio_note: Question-level LR+/LR- is not available; age band and baseline are source-defined routing variables.

source.implementation_notes: Do not use adult mental-status assumptions for children or children with disability.

requiredQuestions.label: Ask headache pattern
requiredQuestions.text: For headache, when did it start, where is it located, how severe is it, is it acute recurrent or chronic/progressive, and what triggers or relieves it?
requiredQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask when headache, vomiting with headache, visual symptoms, or caregiver concern for headache is present.
requiredQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Distinguishes primary headache patterns from chronic progressive, sudden severe, positional, occipital, or sleep-related secondary headache patterns.
requiredQuestions.management_implication: Progressive, positional, sudden severe, occipital, morning, sleep-waking, or activity-limiting patterns change senior review, imaging discussion, follow-up, and discharge safety.
requiredQuestions.options: Multi-select: sudden severe / progressive worsening / early morning / wakes from sleep / cough sneeze or bend trigger / positional / occipital / recurrent nonprogressive / activity-limiting / none of these / unable to obtain
requiredQuestions.likelihood_ratio_note: Question-level LR+/LR- is not available; RCH/NICE red-flag patterns change urgency rather than providing one LR.

source.implementation_notes: Keep pattern history atomic and multi-select rather than a generic headache HPI paragraph.

requiredQuestions.label: Ask headache-associated danger features
requiredQuestions.text: With headache, are vomiting without another clear cause, ataxia, squint or upward-gaze difficulty, visual change, papilledema concern, confusion, lethargy, focal neurologic symptoms, seizure, fever with meningism, recent head injury, shunt, malignancy, immunosuppression, hypertension, bleeding tendency, or anticoagulant/antiplatelet use present?
requiredQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask in every pediatric headache or suspected raised-ICP presentation.
requiredQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Identifies source-defined same-day assessment, urgent referral, imaging, infection, head-injury, and specialty-risk features.
requiredQuestions.management_implication: Any positive feature changes senior review, imaging/LP safety discussion, infection/head-injury pathway, admission/transfer threshold, and discharge constraints.
requiredQuestions.options: Multi-select: vomiting / ataxia / abnormal eye movement or squint / visual change / papilledema concern / confusion or lethargy / focal neurologic symptom / seizure / fever with neck stiffness or photophobia / recent head injury / VP shunt / malignancy or immunosuppression / hypertension / bleeding tendency or anticoagulant / none / unable to obtain
requiredQuestions.likelihood_ratio_note: Question-level LR+/LR- is not available for this red-flag cluster; each positive feature changes management by guideline rule.

source.source_section: headaches in children under 12 and recurrent headache recommendations
source.implementation_notes: Under-12 same-day criteria and under-4 urgent neuro assessment rules must remain visible.

requiredQuestions.label: Ask seizure or blackout event details
requiredQuestions.text: For seizure, blackout, or altered event, how did it start, how long did it last, was it focal or generalized, was there full recovery between events, were rescue medicines given, and is the child still seizing?
requiredQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask for seizure, blackout with seizure markers, vacant spells, altered event, or unexplained collapse.
requiredQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Distinguishes status epilepticus, first seizure, focal seizure, complex febrile seizure, syncope mimic, and recurrent epilepsy.
requiredQuestions.management_implication: Ongoing seizure, duration over 5 minutes, repeated events without recovery, focal onset, rescue medicines, or incomplete recovery changes emergency medication, glucose/labs, imaging, admission, transfer, and follow-up.
requiredQuestions.options: Multi-select: still seizing / lasted over 5 minutes / repeated without recovery / focal onset or focal signs / generalized convulsion / vacant spell or altered awareness / rescue medication given / first seizure / known epilepsy typical event / full recovery / no seizure markers / unable to obtain
requiredQuestions.likelihood_ratio_note: Question-level LR+/LR- is not applicable to status epilepticus; duration and recovery define emergency management.

source.implementation_notes: Active seizure should bypass routine workflow and trigger emergency protocol.

requiredQuestions.label: Ask recovery and focal deficit status
requiredQuestions.text: After the event, has the child returned to their usual baseline, and are persistent weakness, facial droop, aphasia, vision change, diplopia, ataxia, severe headache, or behavior change present?
requiredQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask after seizure, blackout, head injury, altered consciousness, or any focal neurologic concern.
requiredQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Identifies post-ictal recovery, persistent focal signs, pediatric stroke concern, raised ICP, infection, trauma, or metabolic/toxic causes.
requiredQuestions.management_implication: Incomplete recovery or persistent focal findings change glucose/labs, imaging, EEG/referral, senior review, admission, transfer, and discharge safety.
requiredQuestions.options: Multi-select: returned to baseline / not at baseline / persistent weakness / facial droop / aphasia or speech change / vision change or diplopia / ataxia / severe headache / behavior change / none of these / unable to assess
requiredQuestions.likelihood_ratio_note: Question-level LR+/LR- is not available; persistent deficits are management-changing red flags.

source.implementation_notes: Persistent focal findings after the post-ictal phase should not be hidden under a generic seizure label.

requiredQuestions.label: Ask fever meningitis encephalitis features
requiredQuestions.text: Are fever, neck stiffness, photophobia, rash or petechiae, severe irritability, persistent vomiting, immunization gap, sick contacts, travel, recent antibiotics, or concern for meningitis/encephalitis/sepsis present?
requiredQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask when headache, seizure, altered consciousness, fever, rash, or systemic illness is present.
requiredQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Identifies meningitis, encephalitis, sepsis, intracranial abscess, febrile seizure context, and infection-source routing.
requiredQuestions.management_implication: Positive features change isolation, cultures/labs, antibiotics/antivirals, LP safety, imaging, senior review, admission, and transfer.
requiredQuestions.options: Multi-select: fever / neck stiffness / photophobia / petechiae or purpura / severe irritability / persistent vomiting / immunization gap / sick contacts / travel / recent antibiotics / none of these / unable to obtain
requiredQuestions.likelihood_ratio_note: Question-level LR+/LR- is not available for this infection cluster; source features trigger infection and sepsis pathways.

source.implementation_notes: Do not perform LP in a child with reduced GCS; stabilize and follow local infection pathway.

requiredQuestions.label: Ask trauma toxin metabolic context
requiredQuestions.text: Has there been head injury, fall, possible non-accidental injury, poisoning or drug exposure, alcohol or substance use, diabetes, poor intake, dehydration, renal/hepatic disease, hypothermia, inborn-error/metabolic condition, or medication overdose possibility?
requiredQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask for altered consciousness, seizure, severe headache, unexplained behavior change, or neurologic deficit.
requiredQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Identifies traumatic, safeguarding, toxicologic, hypoglycemic, DKA/electrolyte, hepatic/renal, and metabolic causes.
requiredQuestions.management_implication: Positive features change glucose/labs/toxicology, head injury imaging, safeguarding review, poison center contact, DKA/metabolic workup, senior review, admission, and transfer.
requiredQuestions.options: Multi-select: head injury or fall / possible non-accidental injury / poisoning or medication exposure / alcohol or substance use / diabetes / poor intake or dehydration / renal or hepatic disease / hypothermia / known metabolic condition / overdose possible / none of these / unable to obtain confidentially
requiredQuestions.likelihood_ratio_note: Question-level LR+/LR- is not available; these are source-defined causes that redirect management.

source.implementation_notes: Use confidential adolescent history and local safeguarding policy where relevant.

conditionalQuestions.label: Ask under-4 head size and raised-ICP context
conditionalQuestions.text: For a child under 4 years or concern for abnormal head shape/size, has head circumference changed across centile lines, or are tense fontanelle, vomiting, unsteadiness, sixth-nerve palsy, upward-gaze failure, or headache present?
conditionalQuestions.options: Multi-select: head circumference crossing centiles / tense fontanelle / vomiting / unsteadiness or ataxia / sixth nerve palsy / upward-gaze failure / headache / none / not applicable / unable to measure
conditionalQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask when the child is under 4 years, has abnormal head shape/size concern, or has raised-ICP features.
conditionalQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Identifies NICE urgent pediatric/neuro assessment and imaging triggers in younger children.
conditionalQuestions.management_implication: Positive features change urgent pediatric assessment, cranial imaging, senior review, admission, transfer, and discharge safety.

source.source_section: headaches under 4 and head shape or size abnormalities
source.implementation_notes: Do not apply adult headache reassurance to children under 4 with headache.

conditionalQuestions.likelihood_ratio_note: Question-level LR+/LR- is not available; NICE uses these findings as referral/imaging triggers.

conditionalQuestions.label: Ask known epilepsy and rescue plan
conditionalQuestions.text: For known epilepsy or recurrent seizures, is this a typical event, is there a seizure management plan, have rescue medicines been used, are medicines missed, has sleep illness or trigger changed, and are seizures more frequent or uncontrolled?
conditionalQuestions.options: Multi-select: typical event / atypical event / seizure plan available / rescue medication used / missed antiseizure medicine / illness or sleep trigger / more frequent or uncontrolled / no known epilepsy / unable to obtain
conditionalQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask when known epilepsy, recurrent seizure, rescue medicine, or seizure plan is mentioned.
conditionalQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Distinguishes typical breakthrough seizure from uncontrolled epilepsy, medication issue, acute illness, or atypical event requiring review.
conditionalQuestions.management_implication: Atypical, frequent, uncontrolled, no plan, or incomplete recovery changes medication review, labs, EEG/neuro follow-up, admission, and discharge education.

source.implementation_notes: Avoid CT in established epilepsy after a typical seizure unless other indications are present.

conditionalQuestions.likelihood_ratio_note: Question-level LR+/LR- is not available; event typicality and recovery change imaging and consultation.

conditionalQuestions.label: Ask adolescent confidential neuro context
conditionalQuestions.text: For an adolescent, are pregnancy possible, postpartum state, estrogen therapy, substance use, eating disorder, migraine triggers, bullying/stress, medication overuse, self-harm, or unsafe environment relevant?
conditionalQuestions.options: Multi-select: pregnancy possible / postpartum / estrogen therapy / substance use / eating disorder / stress or bullying / medication overuse / self-harm concern / unsafe environment / none endorsed / unable to obtain confidentially
conditionalQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask confidentially for adolescents with headache, seizure, altered mental status, syncope mimic, or focal neurologic concern.
conditionalQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Identifies pregnancy/medication/imaging constraints, substance/toxin causes, medication-overuse headache, psychosocial triggers, and safety risks.
conditionalQuestions.management_implication: Positive context changes pregnancy testing, medication choice, toxin workup, mental-health/safeguarding review, follow-up, and specialty routing.

source.source_section: other features and adolescent psychosocial factors
source.implementation_notes: Use local confidential adolescent-care policy and avoid PHI-rich export.

conditionalQuestions.likelihood_ratio_note: Question-level LR+/LR- is not applicable to confidential context; positive context changes safety and medication choices.

conditionalQuestions.label: Ask shunt bleeding immune-risk context
conditionalQuestions.text: Is there a VP shunt, neurosurgery, malignancy, immunosuppression, bleeding disorder, anticoagulant/antiplatelet use, prothrombotic condition, sickle cell disease, or severe anemia?
conditionalQuestions.options: Multi-select: VP shunt / recent neurosurgery / malignancy / immunosuppression / bleeding disorder / anticoagulant or antiplatelet / prothrombotic condition / sickle cell disease / severe anemia / none / unable to obtain
conditionalQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask when headache, seizure, focal deficit, altered mental status, trauma, or systemic disease context is present.
conditionalQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Identifies higher-risk intracranial, infectious, hemorrhagic, thrombotic, or hematologic pathways.
conditionalQuestions.management_implication: Positive context changes imaging, cultures/labs, specialty consultation, anticoagulation safety, transfusion/hematology review, admission, and transfer.

source.source_section: red flags: systemic disorder, medications, and VP shunt
source.implementation_notes: These risk contexts should appear as limitations and escalation triggers, not hidden metadata.

conditionalQuestions.likelihood_ratio_note: Question-level LR+/LR- is not available; these contexts change threshold for urgent workup.

requiredExam.label: Inspect pupils
requiredExam.technique: Inspect pupil size, symmetry, and reactivity to light.
requiredExam.findings_options: Equal reactive / unequal / fixed or poorly reactive / dilated / pinpoint / unable to assess
requiredExam.when_to_perform: Perform in every pediatric headache, seizure, altered mental status, or focal neurologic concern when safe.
requiredExam.diagnostic_target: Raised intracranial pressure, herniation risk, third nerve palsy, toxin effect, or focal neurologic lesion.
requiredExam.likelihood_ratio_note: No maneuver-specific LR+/LR- is available; abnormal pupils are source-backed raised-ICP or toxin/focal-neuro clues.
requiredExam.management_change: Abnormal pupils change urgent senior review, imaging, airway/resuscitation, toxin evaluation, and transfer threshold.
requiredExam.limitations: Ambient light, medications, trauma, and cooperation affect assessment.

source.source_section: examination: signs of raised intracranial pressure
source.implementation_notes: This is an individual bedside maneuver, not a generic neurologic exam bundle.

requiredExam.label: Inspect eye movements
requiredExam.technique: Ask the child to follow a target horizontally and vertically when developmentally able; observe for squint, sixth-nerve palsy, diplopia, nystagmus, or upward-gaze limitation.
requiredExam.findings_options: normal / squint / sixth nerve palsy concern / diplopia / nystagmus / upward-gaze limitation / unable to assess
requiredExam.when_to_perform: Perform when headache, visual symptoms, raised-ICP concern, focal symptoms, or altered mental status is present.
requiredExam.diagnostic_target: Raised intracranial pressure, cranial neuropathy, brainstem/cerebellar process, migraine aura, or focal lesion.
requiredExam.likelihood_ratio_note: No maneuver-specific LR+/LR- is available; abnormal eye movement findings are guideline-defined red flags.
requiredExam.management_change: Abnormal findings change urgent imaging/senior review, neurology or ophthalmology discussion, and discharge safety.
requiredExam.limitations: Age, development, cooperation, and visual acuity affect interpretation.

source.source_section: headache and raised intracranial pressure referral features
source.implementation_notes: Failure of upward gaze or squint with headache is not a benign migraine feature.

requiredExam.label: Test limb strength
requiredExam.technique: Test age-appropriate antigravity and resisted strength in each limb, including pronator drift when able.
requiredExam.findings_options: symmetric strength / unilateral weakness / bilateral weakness / pronator drift / posturing / unable to assess
requiredExam.when_to_perform: Perform when focal symptoms, seizure recovery, altered mental status, headache red flags, or stroke concern is present.
requiredExam.diagnostic_target: Persistent focal neurologic deficit, stroke, intracranial lesion, Todd paresis, spinal process, or generalized weakness.
requiredExam.likelihood_ratio_note: No maneuver-specific LR+/LR- is available; persistent focal weakness changes management regardless of adult scoring.
requiredExam.management_change: Abnormal strength changes glucose/metabolic correction, imaging, neurology/senior review, admission, and transfer threshold.
requiredExam.limitations: Pain, developmental stage, cooperation, post-ictal state, and baseline disability affect interpretation.

source.implementation_notes: Document whether weakness persists after expected post-ictal recovery.

requiredExam.label: Test coordination or gait when safe
requiredExam.technique: When safe and developmentally appropriate, observe sitting balance, reaching, finger-nose testing, tandem or normal gait, and truncal stability.
requiredExam.findings_options: normal / ataxia / wide-based gait / truncal instability / unable or unsafe to test / baseline differs
requiredExam.when_to_perform: Perform when headache, dizziness/vertigo, focal symptoms, seizure recovery, or altered mental status is present and the child can be tested safely.
requiredExam.diagnostic_target: Ataxia, cerebellar process, raised intracranial pressure, intoxication, posterior circulation process, or gait-threatening neurologic disease.
requiredExam.likelihood_ratio_note: No maneuver-specific LR+/LR- is available; ataxia is a source-defined red flag in pediatric headache.
requiredExam.management_change: Ataxia or unsafe gait changes senior review, imaging, toxin/metabolic workup, admission, transfer, and discharge safety.
requiredExam.limitations: Do not force unsafe gait testing; fatigue, medications, baseline disability, and developmental stage affect interpretation.

source.implementation_notes: Gait/coordination is an individual maneuver, not a generic broad neuro exam label.

requiredExam.label: Inspect optic discs by fundoscopy
requiredExam.technique: Perform or request fundoscopy to assess for papilledema when recurrent headache, red flags, visual symptoms, raised-ICP concern, or local capability allows.
requiredExam.findings_options: normal disc / papilledema concern / unable to visualize / deferred to clinician with equipment / not feasible
requiredExam.when_to_perform: Perform or request in recurrent headache and any raised-ICP concern; document if not feasible.
requiredExam.diagnostic_target: Papilledema, raised intracranial pressure, optic nerve abnormality, or urgent neuroimaging trigger.
requiredExam.likelihood_ratio_note: No maneuver-specific LR+/LR- is available; NICE and RCH treat papilledema/abnormal fundoscopy as urgent.
requiredExam.management_change: Papilledema or inability to assess with red flags changes urgent senior review, neuroimaging, ophthalmology/neurology route, and discharge safety.
requiredExam.limitations: Bedside fundoscopy can be difficult; a failed exam does not rule out papilledema.

source.implementation_notes: Expose fundoscopy as a specific maneuver, not hidden inside broad neuro exam.

conditionalExam.label: Test neck stiffness
conditionalExam.technique: Assess for neck stiffness or meningism in an age-appropriate way without forcing painful movement in an unstable child.
conditionalExam.findings_options: absent / present / photophobia present / not tolerated / unsafe to assess
conditionalExam.when_to_perform: Perform when fever, headache, photophobia, rash, altered mental status, or meningitis/encephalitis concern is present.
conditionalExam.diagnostic_target: Meningitis, encephalitis, sepsis, raised intracranial pressure, or other CNS infection.
conditionalExam.likelihood_ratio_note: No maneuver-specific LR+/LR- is available; meningism changes infection and LP-safety pathway.
conditionalExam.management_change: Positive or concerning findings change cultures/labs, antibiotics/antivirals, LP safety, imaging, admission, and transfer.
conditionalExam.limitations: Young age, irritability, analgesia, and altered mental status can limit reliability.

source.source_section: red flag findings: signs of meningism
source.implementation_notes: Do not delay antibiotics/antivirals if suspected CNS infection and the child is unwell.

conditionalExam.label: Inspect skin for rash or bruising
conditionalExam.technique: Inspect exposed skin for petechiae, purpura, bruising, birth marks, sweating, needle marks, or neurocutaneous stigmata.
conditionalExam.findings_options: none / petechiae or purpura / bruising / neurocutaneous stigmata / sweating or needle marks / unable to assess
conditionalExam.when_to_perform: Perform when fever, altered mental status, seizure, headache red flags, bruising, toxin, or safeguarding concern is present.
conditionalExam.diagnostic_target: Sepsis/meningococcemia, bleeding disorder, non-accidental injury, toxin exposure, neurocutaneous syndrome, or systemic disease.
conditionalExam.likelihood_ratio_note: No maneuver-specific LR+/LR- is available; positive findings change management by source-defined risk.
conditionalExam.management_change: Positive findings change sepsis/rash pathway, cultures/labs, safeguarding review, hematology workup, toxicology review, and disposition.
conditionalExam.limitations: Respect privacy and document if full skin inspection is deferred.

source.implementation_notes: Route prominent rash to the pediatric rash/skin module.

conditionalExam.label: Inspect head for trauma signs
conditionalExam.technique: Inspect scalp, face, ears, and nose for hematoma, bruising, CSF-like fluid, blood, mastoid signs, or concerning injury pattern.
conditionalExam.findings_options: none / scalp hematoma / bruising / ear or nasal blood or clear fluid / mastoid signs / injury pattern concern / unable to assess
conditionalExam.when_to_perform: Perform when head injury, seizure or amnesia after injury, vomiting, altered mental status, or safeguarding concern is present.
conditionalExam.diagnostic_target: Head injury, skull fracture, intracranial injury, CSF leak, non-accidental injury, or infection source.
conditionalExam.likelihood_ratio_note: No maneuver-specific LR+/LR- is available here; head injury findings route to local head injury imaging and observation rules.
conditionalExam.management_change: Positive findings change head injury pathway, imaging, safeguarding review, admission, observation, and transfer.
conditionalExam.limitations: A normal external exam does not exclude intracranial injury.

source.implementation_notes: Use local head injury and child-safety pathways when positive.

conditionalExam.label: Measure head circumference
conditionalExam.technique: For children under 4 years with abnormal head size/shape or raised-ICP concern, take 3 head-circumference measurements and plot the longest on an appropriate chart.
conditionalExam.findings_options: normal trend / below 2nd centile / crosses 2 or more centile lines / rapidly increasing / unable to measure / not applicable
conditionalExam.when_to_perform: Perform for children under 4 with suspected abnormal head shape/size or raised-ICP concern.
conditionalExam.diagnostic_target: Hydrocephalus, microcephaly, progressive raised intracranial pressure, or abnormal head growth.
conditionalExam.likelihood_ratio_note: No maneuver-specific LR+/LR- is available; NICE defines centile changes as referral/imaging triggers.
conditionalExam.management_change: Abnormal measurement changes urgent pediatric assessment, cranial imaging, follow-up, admission, and transfer.
conditionalExam.limitations: Measurement technique and chart selection affect interpretation.

source.source_section: head shape or size abnormalities under 4 years
source.implementation_notes: This is an individual measurement, not a generic pediatric exam.

redFlags.label: Seizure over 5 minutes or repeated without recovery
redFlags.action: Treat active seizure lasting more than 5 minutes or repeated seizures without full recovery as status epilepticus until controlled.
redFlags.rationale: CHQ defines status epilepticus this way and notes it is a neurologic emergency with rising refractory risk as seizure duration increases.
redFlags.management_change: Changes airway/breathing/circulation priority, glucose check, benzodiazepine timing, second-line therapy, senior/PICU/retrieval escalation, and transfer.
redFlags.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable; the time/recovery threshold defines emergency management.
redFlags.limitations: Include prehospital medication doses and seizure duration when deciding next steps.

source.implementation_notes: This red flag should bypass routine build pacing and prompt emergency protocol.

redFlags.label: Persistent altered conscious state
redFlags.action: Treat persistent or unexplained altered conscious state, GCS below 15, abnormal AVPU, or failure to return to baseline as high risk.
redFlags.rationale: RCH links pediatric altered conscious state with significant morbidity/mortality and requires a cause-directed urgent workup.
redFlags.diagnostic_target: Infection, trauma, seizure, poisoning, metabolic disorder, hypoxia, shock, stroke, hemorrhage, shunt complication, or safeguarding concern.
redFlags.management_change: Changes POCT glucose, cultures/labs/toxicology, imaging, LP safety, antibiotics/antivirals, senior review, admission, and transfer.
redFlags.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable; persistence and unexplained altered baseline are management-changing safety features.
redFlags.limitations: Post-ictal state and sedating medicines can explain but do not automatically make persistent altered consciousness safe.

source.source_section: key points, initial assessment, discharge criteria
source.implementation_notes: Discharge requires return to normal conscious state and a clear follow-up plan.

redFlags.label: Pediatric headache red flags
redFlags.action: Escalate headache with under-4 age, morning or sleep-waking pattern, progressive worsening, cough/positional trigger, sudden severe onset, occipital location, unexplained vomiting, focal symptoms, seizure, regression, meningism, recent head injury, VP shunt, systemic disease, hypertension, or anticoagulant/bleeding risk.
redFlags.rationale: RCH and NICE list these as pediatric headache red flags requiring senior review, urgent referral, same-day assessment, or imaging consideration.
redFlags.diagnostic_target: Raised intracranial pressure, intracranial mass, hydrocephalus, meningitis, encephalitis, abscess, head injury, shunt failure, hypertension, stroke, or systemic disease.
redFlags.management_change: Changes same-day assessment, senior review, imaging discussion, infection/head-injury routing, admission, transfer, and discharge restrictions.
redFlags.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not available for the combined red-flag set; each feature changes management by guideline rule.
redFlags.limitations: Normal pain relief does not remove red flags such as papilledema, focal deficit, or progressive pattern.

source.implementation_notes: Red flags must be visible, not buried in generic headache prose.

redFlags.label: Focal deficit, papilledema, or raised-ICP signs
redFlags.action: Escalate new focal neurologic abnormality, papilledema, ataxia, abnormal pupils, abnormal posturing, falling GCS, irregular respirations, or hypertension with bradycardia.
redFlags.rationale: RCH identifies these as red-flag neurologic or raised-ICP findings in pediatric headache and altered conscious state.
redFlags.diagnostic_target: Raised intracranial pressure, herniation risk, intracranial lesion, stroke, hemorrhage, infection, or seizure focus.
redFlags.management_change: Changes urgent imaging, senior/neurology review, resuscitation, LP avoidance until safe, admission, transfer, and monitoring.
redFlags.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not available; these findings are high-risk examination triggers.
redFlags.limitations: Cushing reflex is late; do not wait for it before escalating other raised-ICP signs.

source.source_section: examination: signs of raised intracranial pressure
source.implementation_notes: Do not perform LP in a reduced-GCS child.

redFlags.label: CNS infection, trauma, toxin, or safeguarding concern
redFlags.action: Escalate fever with meningism or petechiae, suspected CNS infection, significant head injury, poisoning/toxin exposure, child abuse concern, shunt complication, immunocompromise, bleeding disorder, or anticoagulant exposure.
redFlags.rationale: RCH altered-conscious-state guidance lists infection, trauma, poisoning, metabolic disease, and child abuse as important pediatric causes requiring cause-specific urgent management.
redFlags.diagnostic_target: Meningitis, encephalitis, sepsis, head injury, intracranial hemorrhage, poisoning, safeguarding injury, shunt failure, or systemic disease.
redFlags.management_change: Changes cultures/labs, antimicrobials/antivirals, toxicology and poison-center route, imaging, safeguarding review, admission, transfer, and discharge safety.
redFlags.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not available for this broad safety frame; source-defined causes determine urgent management.
redFlags.limitations: Absence of a single feature does not rule out CNS infection, occult injury, or poisoning.

source.source_section: causes, history, investigations, treatment, and consultation
source.implementation_notes: Use local sepsis, meningitis, head injury, poisoning, and child-safety pathways immediately when indicated.

initialTests.label: Do not order routine tests for low-risk headache
initialTests.action: Avoid routine labs or imaging for pediatric headache without red flags and with normal examination.
initialTests.rationale: RCH states investigations are not routinely indicated for children with headache who have no red-flag features on history or examination.
initialTests.diagnostic_target: Low-risk primary headache or migraine after secondary headache red flags are absent.
initialTests.when_to_order: Use only after red flags are absent, BP and neurologic examination are reassuring, and follow-up/safety net is reliable.
initialTests.management_change: Avoids unnecessary imaging/labs and supports symptom care, trigger review, medication-overuse review, follow-up, and return precautions.
initialTests.reference_range_or_threshold: Red flags absent; normal neurologic exam; BP not concerning for age/height; no under-4 urgent referral trigger.
initialTests.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to a testing-restraint rule.
initialTests.limitations: Do not apply if any source-defined red flag or abnormal exam is present.

source.implementation_notes: Testing restraint is a positive recommendation, not missing care.

initialTests.label: Point-of-care glucose
initialTests.rationale: Hypoglycemia can cause seizures or altered conscious state and requires immediate correction.
initialTests.diagnostic_target: Hypoglycemia, hyperglycemia, DKA, or metabolic mimic of seizure or altered mental status.
initialTests.when_to_order: Order immediately for seizure, altered conscious state, unexplained focal deficit, weakness, poor intake, diabetes, toxic/metabolic concern, or any unstable neuro presentation.
initialTests.management_change: Abnormal result changes immediate dextrose or hyperglycemia/DKA pathway, seizure control, observation, admission, and discharge safety.
initialTests.reference_range_or_threshold: Use local pediatric glucose units and hypoglycemia threshold; CHQ status epilepticus pathway specifically flags glucose <=2.6 mmol/L for hypoglycemia management.
initialTests.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable; glucose is a directly management-changing bedside result.
initialTests.limitations: Confirm unexpected results where feasible without delaying treatment.

source.implementation_notes: Do not wait for formal labs when bedside glucose is needed.

initialTests.label: Seizure metabolic labs when indicated
initialTests.action: Order electrolytes, calcium, venous gas, renal/liver function, and other metabolic labs guided by seizure or altered-conscious-state context.
initialTests.rationale: RCH recommends considering electrolytes, calcium, and venous gas when seizure requires a second-line agent, child is under 12 months, comorbidity exists, dehydration/toxidrome is possible, or baseline has not returned.
initialTests.diagnostic_target: Electrolyte disturbance, hypocalcemia, acid-base disorder, renal/hepatic dysfunction, dehydration, diabetes-related metabolic crisis, or toxin/metabolic seizure trigger.
initialTests.when_to_order: Order when seizure needed second-line agent, age under 12 months, metabolic disorder/diabetes/dehydration/toxidrome is possible, or the child has not returned to baseline after post-ictal phase and medication effects.
initialTests.management_change: Results change electrolyte/glucose/metabolic correction, DKA/toxin pathway, admission, ICU/transfer, and specialty consultation.
initialTests.reference_range_or_threshold: Use age-appropriate local lab ranges; correct dangerous sodium, calcium, glucose, acid-base, renal, or hepatic abnormalities urgently.
initialTests.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not assigned to this conditional lab bundle; results are management-changing when source-defined indications are present.
initialTests.limitations: Do not delay seizure termination or airway support to obtain labs.

source.implementation_notes: This should not become routine labs for every resolved simple event.

initialTests.label: 12-lead ECG when seizure mimic or first seizure assessment
initialTests.action: Obtain 12-lead ECG with rhythm and interval review.
initialTests.rationale: NICE recommends ECG after first suspected seizure to help identify cardiac conditions that can mimic epilepsy.
initialTests.diagnostic_target: Arrhythmia, long QT, pre-excitation, conduction disease, myocarditis/pericarditis clue, or cardiac mimic of seizure/blackout.
initialTests.when_to_order: Order for first suspected seizure, blackout where seizure versus syncope is unclear, palpitations, exertional event, family sudden death, or arrhythmia/toxin concern.
initialTests.management_change: Abnormal ECG changes cardiology review, monitoring, medication/toxin management, admission, and activity/discharge restrictions.
initialTests.reference_range_or_threshold: Use pediatric ECG interpretation; assess rhythm, QTc, pre-excitation, conduction, ischemia/myocarditis clues, and local age-specific thresholds.
initialTests.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not assigned; ECG identifies mimics and management-changing rhythm/interval abnormalities.
initialTests.limitations: Normal ECG does not exclude epilepsy or all arrhythmias.

source.implementation_notes: Route dominant syncope/chest symptoms to the pediatric chest pain/syncope module.

initialTests.label: Neuroimaging when indicated
initialTests.action: Discuss or obtain urgent CT or MRI per local pediatric imaging pathway after stabilization; use MRI for epilepsy structural evaluation when specialist diagnosis requires it.
initialTests.rationale: RCH seizure and altered-conscious-state guidance list trauma, focal seizure, third-line seizure therapy, age under 6 months, elevated ICP signs, new focal signs, bleeding/anticoagulation, or failure to return to baseline as acute imaging triggers.
initialTests.diagnostic_target: Raised intracranial pressure, intracranial mass, hydrocephalus, hemorrhage, stroke, trauma, infection complication, structural epilepsy cause, or shunt complication.
initialTests.when_to_order: Order or discuss urgently when headache red flags, raised ICP signs, trauma, focal seizure/deficit, age under 6 months with seizure, bleeding/anticoagulation, persistent altered state, or stroke concern is present.
initialTests.management_change: Findings change neurology/neurosurgery consultation, antibiotics/antivirals, seizure care, head injury pathway, admission, transfer, and LP safety.
initialTests.reference_range_or_threshold: Image for source-defined red flags; avoid CT for established epilepsy after a typical seizure unless other concerns exist.
initialTests.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not assigned to imaging selection; the indication is source- and risk-feature driven.
initialTests.limitations: Do not delay resuscitation, seizure control, or urgent antimicrobials for imaging.

source.implementation_notes: Show imaging indications and restraint together to avoid CT overuse.

initialTests.label: EEG and urgent first-seizure referral
initialTests.action: Arrange urgent pediatric seizure assessment and EEG planning when indicated.
initialTests.rationale: NICE recommends urgent assessment within 2 weeks after first suspected seizure, and RCH notes EEG is not routine acutely after first seizure but should be considered in children under 12 months or with known genetic condition.
initialTests.diagnostic_target: First suspected seizure, epilepsy syndrome, recurrent afebrile seizure risk, seizure classification, or diagnostic uncertainty.
initialTests.when_to_order: Arrange urgent specialist assessment after first suspected seizure; consider EEG if epilepsy is suspected, after second seizure, under 12 months, known genetic condition, or specialist pathway requests it.
initialTests.management_change: Changes diagnosis, seizure-type classification, family safety counseling, medicine decisions, follow-up timing, and need for MRI or genetic testing.
initialTests.reference_range_or_threshold: NICE: urgent assessment after first suspected seizure within 2 weeks; if EEG requested after first seizure, perform as soon as possible, ideally within 72 hours.
initialTests.likelihood_ratio_note: EEG should support diagnosis but should not be used alone to exclude epilepsy.
initialTests.limitations: EEG is rarely required in the acute ED setting and a normal EEG does not rule out epilepsy.

source.source_section: referral after first seizure and EEG recommendations
source.implementation_notes: Keep EEG as a follow-up/diagnostic support route rather than an acute blanket test.

initialTests.label: CNS infection testing with LP safety check
initialTests.action: Order CBC, renal/liver function, blood gas, blood/urine cultures, and lumbar puncture only when clinically stable and safe, guided by suspected infection.
initialTests.rationale: RCH altered-conscious-state guidance recommends cause-directed tests and states lumbar puncture should not be performed in a child with reduced GCS.
initialTests.diagnostic_target: Meningitis, encephalitis, sepsis, intracranial infection, metabolic complication, or infection-related altered conscious state.
initialTests.when_to_order: Order when fever, meningism, encephalitis, sepsis, immunocompromise, persistent altered state, or infection source is suspected; withhold LP until stable and safe.
initialTests.management_change: Results change antibiotics/antivirals, sepsis care, admission, transfer, isolation, and specialty consultation.
initialTests.reference_range_or_threshold: Reduced GCS: do not perform LP; give urgent antimicrobials/antivirals for suspected infective cause per local guideline.
initialTests.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not assigned to this infection-testing bundle; source-defined safety and treatment rules drive management.
initialTests.limitations: Do not delay antimicrobials/antivirals for LP or imaging in an unwell child.

source.implementation_notes: LP safety must be explicit to prevent unsafe recommendation text.

dispositionRules.label: Active seizure emergency route
dispositionRules.action: If the child is actively seizing, has seizure over 5 minutes, or has repeated seizures without recovery, activate local pediatric status epilepticus protocol and senior/critical-care support.
dispositionRules.rationale: Status epilepticus is a neurologic emergency and risk of refractory seizure increases with seizure duration.
dispositionRules.diagnostic_target: Status epilepticus, refractory seizure, airway compromise, hypoglycemia, or ongoing neurologic injury risk.
dispositionRules.management_change: Changes airway support, glucose check, benzodiazepine/second-line therapy, monitoring, ICU/retrieval discussion, and transfer.
dispositionRules.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to this management rule; seizure duration and recovery define emergency treatment.
dispositionRules.limitations: This rule does not replace local status epilepticus medication protocol, airway leadership, or PICU/retrieval advice.

source.implementation_notes: Do not wait for a generated checklist before treating active status.

dispositionRules.label: Persistent altered conscious state admission or transfer
dispositionRules.action: Do not discharge a child with persistent or unexplained altered conscious state; consult pediatric/senior services and admit or transfer if needs exceed local capability.
dispositionRules.rationale: RCH discharge criteria require conscious state returned to normal and a clear follow-up plan.
dispositionRules.diagnostic_target: Persistent altered conscious state from infection, trauma, toxin, metabolic disorder, seizure, hypoxia, shock, stroke, hemorrhage, shunt complication, or safeguarding concern.
dispositionRules.management_change: Changes monitoring, cause-directed testing, admission, transfer, and handoff.
dispositionRules.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to this disposition rule; return to baseline and explained cause drive safety.
dispositionRules.limitations: Post-ictal or sedating-medication explanation still requires reassessment and safe trajectory before discharge.

source.implementation_notes: Caregiver-reported baseline change counts.

dispositionRules.label: Headache red flag senior review route
dispositionRules.action: If pediatric headache red flags or abnormal neurologic findings are present, discuss with a senior clinician and consider same-day assessment, imaging, infection/head-injury pathway, admission, or transfer.
dispositionRules.rationale: RCH recommends senior clinician discussion and consideration of intracranial imaging when red flags are present; NICE gives same-day assessment criteria for under-12 headache red flags.
dispositionRules.diagnostic_target: Raised intracranial pressure, intracranial lesion, meningitis, encephalitis, head injury, shunt failure, hypertension, stroke, or systemic disease.
dispositionRules.management_change: Changes imaging, tests, specialty routing, discharge eligibility, and follow-up urgency.
dispositionRules.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to this management rule; guideline-defined red flags determine escalation.
dispositionRules.limitations: Symptom improvement alone does not neutralize papilledema, focal signs, progressive pattern, or under-4 headache.

source.implementation_notes: Under-4 headache should not be treated as routine adult migraine.

dispositionRules.label: First seizure follow-up route
dispositionRules.action: After first suspected seizure, arrange urgent pediatric seizure assessment and provide family seizure first-aid and recurrence instructions; do not label epilepsy or start long-term therapy from generic output alone.
dispositionRules.rationale: NICE recommends urgent assessment after first suspected seizure and RCH states all children with first afebrile seizure should have medical follow-up.
dispositionRules.diagnostic_target: First suspected seizure, recurrent afebrile seizure risk, epilepsy syndrome, seizure mimic, and family safety planning.
dispositionRules.management_change: Changes referral timing, safety counseling, EEG/imaging planning, recurrence instructions, and medication ownership.
dispositionRules.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to this follow-up rule; specialist assessment and event details guide diagnosis.
dispositionRules.limitations: Urgent follow-up does not replace emergency care for persistent altered mental status, focal deficit, recurrent seizure, or active seizure.

source.source_section: referral after first seizure and information/support
source.implementation_notes: Long-term antiseizure treatment decisions require clinician/specialist assessment.

dispositionRules.label: Low-risk headache discharge constraints
dispositionRules.action: Low-risk headache discharge is appropriate only when red flags are absent, neurologic exam and BP are reassuring, pain is manageable, caregiver understands return precautions, and follow-up is clear.
dispositionRules.rationale: RCH supports no routine investigations in headache without red flags while requiring red-flag screening and appropriate management.
dispositionRules.diagnostic_target: Low-risk primary headache or migraine with safe outpatient management conditions met.
dispositionRules.management_change: Changes symptom care, medication-overuse advice, trigger review, school/social impact plan, return precautions, and follow-up.
dispositionRules.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to this discharge rule; safety depends on absent red flags, normal exam, and reliable follow-up.
dispositionRules.limitations: Do not use this rule for under-4 headache without neuro review, abnormal exam, vomiting, altered consciousness, fever with meningism, trauma, shunt, or progressive symptoms.

source.implementation_notes: Discharge constraints should be concise enough for copy/export.

criteria_options.label: Headache imaging/escalation triggers include age <4 years, early-morning/sleep-waking/positional/sudden severe/occipital headache, vomiting, seizure, focal neurologic signs, or progressive pattern
criteria_options.criteria_text: Headache red flags include age <4 years; early morning, waking from sleep, coughing/sneezing/positional trigger, sudden severe, or occipital headache; unexplained vomiting; focal neurologic symptoms; new seizure; developmental regression; meningitis/encephalitis features; significant head injury; or systemic high-risk disorder.

criteria_options.label: Active seizure treatment starts when total seizure duration is >=5 minutes or unknown; include prehospital benzodiazepines
criteria_options.criteria_text: Most pediatric seizures resolve within 5 minutes; pharmacologic management should begin when total seizure duration is >=5 minutes or unknown, and prehospital benzodiazepine doses count toward the maximum of 2 benzodiazepine doses.
criteria_options.source_section: Key points and active seizure management

criteria_options.label: Seizure dosing: midazolam 0.15 mg/kg IV/IM/IO or 0.3 mg/kg buccal/IN max 10 mg; phenytoin 20 mg/kg max 2 g; levetiracetam 40-60 mg/kg max 4.5 g
criteria_options.criteria_text: RCH acute seizure medication table lists midazolam 0.15 mg/kg IV/IM/IO or 0.3 mg/kg buccal/IN (max 10 mg); second-line phenytoin 20 mg/kg IV/IO (max 2 g) or levetiracetam 40-60 mg/kg IV/IO (max 4.5 g).

criteria_options.label: Secondary headache, raised intracranial pressure, infection, or head injury
criteria_options.criteria_text: Secondary headache, raised intracranial pressure, infection, or head injury Screen for age under 4 years, morning or sleep-waking headache, progressive worsening, cough/positional trigger, sudden severe onset, occipital pain, vomiting, focal neurologic symptoms, papilledema, meningism, head injury, shunt, malignancy, immunosuppression, hypertension, anticoagulants, or bleeding tendency. Changes urgency of senior review, neuroimaging discussion, lumbar puncture safety, infection testing, antibiotics/antivirals, head injury pathway, admission, transfer, and discharge constraints. Raised intracranial pressure, tumor, hydrocephalus, meningitis, encephalitis, intracranial abscess, head injury, hypertensive headache, shunt complication, or systemic disease. RCH and NICE define pediatric headache red flags that require senior review, same-day assessment, or consideration of intracranial imaging.
criteria_options.source_section: headaches in children under 12 and recurrent headache recommendations

criteria_options.label: Seizure, status epilepticus, or post-ictal neurologic risk
criteria_options.criteria_text: Seizure, status epilepticus, or post-ictal neurologic risk Determine whether the child is actively seizing, has had repeated seizures without return to baseline, had a first afebrile seizure, has focal features, is under 12 months, has metabolic or toxicologic risk, or has not returned to baseline. Changes airway support, benzodiazepine and second-line therapy timing, glucose testing, labs, ECG, neuroimaging, EEG/referral timing, admission, and transfer threshold. Status epilepticus, first afebrile seizure, complex febrile seizure, focal seizure, metabolic seizure, toxicologic seizure, epilepsy syndrome, or seizure mimic. RCH and CHQ treat seizure lasting more than 5 minutes or repeated seizures without recovery as status epilepticus and a neurologic emergency.
criteria_options.source_section: key points, assessment, and emergency management

criteria_options.label: Seizure metabolic labs when indicated
criteria_options.criteria_text: Seizure metabolic labs when indicated Order electrolytes, calcium, venous gas, renal/liver function, and other metabolic labs guided by seizure or altered-conscious-state context. Results change electrolyte/glucose/metabolic correction, DKA/toxin pathway, admission, ICU/transfer, and specialty consultation. Electrolyte disturbance, hypocalcemia, acid-base disorder, renal/hepatic dysfunction, dehydration, diabetes-related metabolic crisis, or toxin/metabolic seizure trigger. RCH recommends considering electrolytes, calcium, and venous gas when seizure requires a second-line agent, child is under 12 months, comorbidity exists, dehydration/toxidrome is possible, or baseline has not returned.

criteria_options.label: Neuroimaging when indicated
criteria_options.criteria_text: Neuroimaging when indicated Discuss or obtain urgent CT or MRI per local pediatric imaging pathway after stabilization; use MRI for epilepsy structural evaluation when specialist diagnosis requires it. Findings change neurology/neurosurgery consultation, antibiotics/antivirals, seizure care, head injury pathway, admission, transfer, and LP safety. Raised intracranial pressure, intracranial mass, hydrocephalus, hemorrhage, stroke, trauma, infection complication, structural epilepsy cause, or shunt complication. RCH seizure and altered-conscious-state guidance list trauma, focal seizure, third-line seizure therapy, age under 6 months, elevated ICP signs, new focal signs, bleeding/anticoagulation, or failure to return to baseline as acute imaging triggers.

criteria_options.label: EEG and urgent first-seizure referral
criteria_options.criteria_text: EEG and urgent first-seizure referral Arrange urgent pediatric seizure assessment and EEG planning when indicated. Changes diagnosis, seizure-type classification, family safety counseling, medicine decisions, follow-up timing, and need for MRI or genetic testing. First suspected seizure, epilepsy syndrome, recurrent afebrile seizure risk, seizure classification, or diagnostic uncertainty. NICE recommends urgent assessment within 2 weeks after first suspected seizure, and RCH notes EEG is not routine acutely after first seizure but should be considered in children under 12 months or with known genetic condition.
criteria_options.source_section: referral after first seizure and EEG recommendations

criteria_options.label: Seizure over 5 minutes or repeated without recovery
criteria_options.criteria_text: Seizure over 5 minutes or repeated without recovery Treat active seizure lasting more than 5 minutes or repeated seizures without full recovery as status epilepticus until controlled. Changes airway/breathing/circulation priority, glucose check, benzodiazepine timing, second-line therapy, senior/PICU/retrieval escalation, and transfer. Status epilepticus or refractory seizure. CHQ defines status epilepticus this way and notes it is a neurologic emergency with rising refractory risk as seizure duration increases.

criteria_options.label: Active seizure emergency route
criteria_options.criteria_text: Active seizure emergency route If the child is actively seizing, has seizure over 5 minutes, or has repeated seizures without recovery, activate local pediatric status epilepticus protocol and senior/critical-care support. Changes airway support, glucose check, benzodiazepine/second-line therapy, monitoring, ICU/retrieval discussion, and transfer. Status epilepticus, refractory seizure, airway compromise, hypoglycemia, or ongoing neurologic injury risk. Status epilepticus is a neurologic emergency and risk of refractory seizure increases with seizure duration.

criteria_options.label: Active seizure treatment starts when total seizure duration is >=5 minutes or unknown; include prehospital benzodiazepines

criteria_options.label: Seizure dosing: midazolam 0.15 mg/kg IV/IM/IO or 0.3 mg/kg buccal/IN max 10 mg; phenytoin 20 mg/kg max 2 g; levetiracetam 40-60 mg/kg max 4.5 g

criteria_options.label: Seizure, status epilepticus, or post-ictal neurologic risk

criteria_options.label: Seizure metabolic labs when indicated

criteria_options.label: Neuroimaging when indicated

children.action: Document the Pediatric headache, seizure, altered mental status, or focal neurologic concern diagnosis/risk category, named pending-result clinician/service, follow-up interval, and return precautions for worsening symptoms, treatment intolerance, inability to complete treatment, or abnormal pending results.

parallel_actions: review treatment response and adverse effects

guideline_cutoffs.label: Headache imaging/escalation triggers include age <4 years, early-morning/sleep-waking/positional/sudden severe/occipital headache, vomiting, seizure, focal neurologic signs, or progressive pattern
guideline_cutoffs.criteria_text: Headache red flags include age <4 years; early morning, waking from sleep, coughing/sneezing/positional trigger, sudden severe, or occipital headache; unexplained vomiting; focal neurologic symptoms; new seizure; developmental regression; meningitis/encephalitis features; significant head injury; or systemic high-risk disorder.

guideline_cutoffs.label: Active seizure treatment starts when total seizure duration is >=5 minutes or unknown; include prehospital benzodiazepines
guideline_cutoffs.criteria_text: Most pediatric seizures resolve within 5 minutes; pharmacologic management should begin when total seizure duration is >=5 minutes or unknown, and prehospital benzodiazepine doses count toward the maximum of 2 benzodiazepine doses.
guideline_cutoffs.source_section: Key points and active seizure management

guideline_cutoffs.label: Seizure dosing: midazolam 0.15 mg/kg IV/IM/IO or 0.3 mg/kg buccal/IN max 10 mg; phenytoin 20 mg/kg max 2 g; levetiracetam 40-60 mg/kg max 4.5 g
guideline_cutoffs.criteria_text: RCH acute seizure medication table lists midazolam 0.15 mg/kg IV/IM/IO or 0.3 mg/kg buccal/IN (max 10 mg); second-line phenytoin 20 mg/kg IV/IO (max 2 g) or levetiracetam 40-60 mg/kg IV/IO (max 4.5 g).

guideline_cutoffs.label: Secondary headache, raised intracranial pressure, infection, or head injury
guideline_cutoffs.criteria_text: Secondary headache, raised intracranial pressure, infection, or head injury Screen for age under 4 years, morning or sleep-waking headache, progressive worsening, cough/positional trigger, sudden severe onset, occipital pain, vomiting, focal neurologic symptoms, papilledema, meningism, head injury, shunt, malignancy, immunosuppression, hypertension, anticoagulants, or bleeding tendency. Changes urgency of senior review, neuroimaging discussion, lumbar puncture safety, infection testing, antibiotics/antivirals, head injury pathway, admission, transfer, and discharge constraints. Raised intracranial pressure, tumor, hydrocephalus, meningitis, encephalitis, intracranial abscess, head injury, hypertensive headache, shunt complication, or systemic disease. RCH and NICE define pediatric headache red flags that require senior review, same-day assessment, or consideration of intracranial imaging.
guideline_cutoffs.source_section: headaches in children under 12 and recurrent headache recommendations

guideline_cutoffs.label: Seizure, status epilepticus, or post-ictal neurologic risk
guideline_cutoffs.criteria_text: Seizure, status epilepticus, or post-ictal neurologic risk Determine whether the child is actively seizing, has had repeated seizures without return to baseline, had a first afebrile seizure, has focal features, is under 12 months, has metabolic or toxicologic risk, or has not returned to baseline. Changes airway support, benzodiazepine and second-line therapy timing, glucose testing, labs, ECG, neuroimaging, EEG/referral timing, admission, and transfer threshold. Status epilepticus, first afebrile seizure, complex febrile seizure, focal seizure, metabolic seizure, toxicologic seizure, epilepsy syndrome, or seizure mimic. RCH and CHQ treat seizure lasting more than 5 minutes or repeated seizures without recovery as status epilepticus and a neurologic emergency.
guideline_cutoffs.source_section: key points, assessment, and emergency management

guideline_cutoffs.label: Seizure metabolic labs when indicated
guideline_cutoffs.criteria_text: Seizure metabolic labs when indicated Order electrolytes, calcium, venous gas, renal/liver function, and other metabolic labs guided by seizure or altered-conscious-state context. Results change electrolyte/glucose/metabolic correction, DKA/toxin pathway, admission, ICU/transfer, and specialty consultation. Electrolyte disturbance, hypocalcemia, acid-base disorder, renal/hepatic dysfunction, dehydration, diabetes-related metabolic crisis, or toxin/metabolic seizure trigger. RCH recommends considering electrolytes, calcium, and venous gas when seizure requires a second-line agent, child is under 12 months, comorbidity exists, dehydration/toxidrome is possible, or baseline has not returned.

guideline_cutoffs.label: Neuroimaging when indicated
guideline_cutoffs.criteria_text: Neuroimaging when indicated Discuss or obtain urgent CT or MRI per local pediatric imaging pathway after stabilization; use MRI for epilepsy structural evaluation when specialist diagnosis requires it. Findings change neurology/neurosurgery consultation, antibiotics/antivirals, seizure care, head injury pathway, admission, transfer, and LP safety. Raised intracranial pressure, intracranial mass, hydrocephalus, hemorrhage, stroke, trauma, infection complication, structural epilepsy cause, or shunt complication. RCH seizure and altered-conscious-state guidance list trauma, focal seizure, third-line seizure therapy, age under 6 months, elevated ICP signs, new focal signs, bleeding/anticoagulation, or failure to return to baseline as acute imaging triggers.

guideline_cutoffs.label: EEG and urgent first-seizure referral
guideline_cutoffs.criteria_text: EEG and urgent first-seizure referral Arrange urgent pediatric seizure assessment and EEG planning when indicated. Changes diagnosis, seizure-type classification, family safety counseling, medicine decisions, follow-up timing, and need for MRI or genetic testing. First suspected seizure, epilepsy syndrome, recurrent afebrile seizure risk, seizure classification, or diagnostic uncertainty. NICE recommends urgent assessment within 2 weeks after first suspected seizure, and RCH notes EEG is not routine acutely after first seizure but should be considered in children under 12 months or with known genetic condition.
guideline_cutoffs.source_section: referral after first seizure and EEG recommendations

guideline_cutoffs.label: Seizure over 5 minutes or repeated without recovery
guideline_cutoffs.criteria_text: Seizure over 5 minutes or repeated without recovery Treat active seizure lasting more than 5 minutes or repeated seizures without full recovery as status epilepticus until controlled. Changes airway/breathing/circulation priority, glucose check, benzodiazepine timing, second-line therapy, senior/PICU/retrieval escalation, and transfer. Status epilepticus or refractory seizure. CHQ defines status epilepticus this way and notes it is a neurologic emergency with rising refractory risk as seizure duration increases.

guideline_cutoffs.label: Active seizure emergency route
guideline_cutoffs.criteria_text: Active seizure emergency route If the child is actively seizing, has seizure over 5 minutes, or has repeated seizures without recovery, activate local pediatric status epilepticus protocol and senior/critical-care support. Changes airway support, glucose check, benzodiazepine/second-line therapy, monitoring, ICU/retrieval discussion, and transfer. Status epilepticus, refractory seizure, airway compromise, hypoglycemia, or ongoing neurologic injury risk. Status epilepticus is a neurologic emergency and risk of refractory seizure increases with seizure duration.
